1: Psalm 108 is a deliberate composite: vv1-5 = Psalms 57:7-11 and vv6-13 = Psalms 60:5-12. The combination is not accidental — it pairs the individual's confident praise (Ps 57, written by David in a cave fleeing Saul) with the national lament over defeat (Ps 60, written after a battle). נָכוֹן לִבִּי אֱלֹהִים אָשִׁירָה וַאֲזַמְּרָה אַף-כְּבוֹדִי (nākhôn libbî ʾĕlōhîm ʾāshîrāh waʾăzammĕrāh ʾap-kĕbôdî, "My heart is ready/established, O God; I will sing and praise you with all my glory!") — nākhôn libbî: my heart is established/firm (kônen: the word for permanent establishment — Psalms 57:7: the same opening). ʾAp-kĕbôdî: yes, even my glory (the kābôd — the weightiest part of the person, the soul-identity — Psalms 7:5; Psalms 30:12: "that my glory may sing to you and not be silent").
2: עוּרָה הַנֵּבֶל וְכִנּוֹר אָעִירָה שָּׁחַר (ʿûrāh hannêbel wĕkhinnôr ʾāʿîrāh shaḥar, "Awake, harp and lyre! I will be the one to rouse the dawn!") — ʾāʿîrāh shaḥar: "I will awaken/arouse the dawn" — a remarkable reversal of the normal order where the dawn awakens the sleeper. The worshipper rises before dawn to worship, and by singing awakens the dawn itself. The commitment to praise is so fierce that it precedes the most natural orientation-marker of the day (Psalms 57:8: the same phrase). Nêbel wĕkhinnôr: the harp and lyre (the large and small stringed instruments of the temple praise ensemble).
3: אוֹדְךָ בָעַמִּים יְהוָה וַאֲזַמֶּרְךָ בַּלְאֻמִּים (ʾôdĕkā bāʿammîm yĕhwāh waʾăzammĕrekā ballĕʾummîm, "I will praise you, O LORD, among all peoples and sing your praises among the nations!") — the universal scope of David's intended praise: not in the congregation alone but bāʿammîm (among the peoples) and ballĕʾummîm (among the nations). Paul cites the parallel verse Psalms 57:9 in Romans 15:9: "I will praise you among the Gentiles" — demonstrating that the inclusion of the Gentiles in the messianic age was anticipated in David's praise-intention.
4: כִּי-גָדוֹל מֵעַל-שָׁמַיִם חַסְדֶּךָ וְעַד-שְׁחָקִים אֲמִתֶּךָ (kî-gādôl mêʿal-shāmayim ḥasdekā wĕʿad-shĕḥāqîm ʾămittekā, "For your steadfast love towers above the heavens, and your faithfulness reaches to the skies!") — the divine character measured against the largest things available for comparison: ḥesed (steadfast love) exceeds the heavens (mêʿal-shāmayim: above/beyond the sky); ʾĕmet/ʾĕmûnāh (faithfulness) reaches to the clouds (shĕḥāqîm: the clouds/upper atmosphere). The covenant attributes are literally unmeasurable — they exceed the largest spatial categories. Psalms 57:10 has the same verse.
5: רוּמָה עַל-שָׁמַיִם אֱלֹהִים וְעַל כָּל-הָאָרֶץ כְּבוֹדֶךָ (rûmāh ʿal-shāmayim ʾĕlōhîm wĕʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā, "Be lifted high above the heavens, O God! Let your glory fill the whole earth!") — the petition: let the divine kābôd that already exceeds the heavens (v4) be manifest so that the whole earth sees it. Rûmāh: be exalted/raised (the qal imperative — a request to God to make his own exaltation visible). Wĕʿal kol-hāʾāreṣ kĕbôdekā: and over all the earth your glory. The same prayer as Isaiah 6:3 (the whole earth is full of his glory) asked to be realized in historical experience.
6: לְמַעַן יֵחָלְצוּן יְדִידֶיךָ הוֹשִׁיעָה יְמִינְךָ וַעֲנֵנִי (lĕmaʿan yêḥālĕṣûn yĕdîdêkā hôshîʿāh yĕmînĕkā waʿănênî, "Save your beloved ones — rescue us by your mighty hand and answer our prayer!") — yĕdîdêkā: your beloved/loved ones (yādîd: beloved — the same root as David's alternate name Jedidiah — 2 Samuel 12:25; Psalms 45:1: the Beloved King; Psalms 84:1: the lovely/beloved dwelling). The beloved community cries for rescue. Hôshîʿāh yĕmînĕkā: save with your right hand — the same right hand that will "make enemies a footstool" (Psalms 110:1).
7: אֱלֹהִים דִּבֶּר בְּקָדְשׁוֹ אֶעְלֹזָה אֲחַלְּקָה שְׁכֶם וְעֵמֶק סֻכּוֹת אֲמַדֵּד (ʾĕlōhîm dibbêr bĕqōdshô ʾeʿlōzāh ʾăḥallĕqāh shĕkhem wĕʿêmeq sukkôt ʾămaddêd, "God has spoken from his holy place: 'I will celebrate; I will apportion Shechem and measure out the valley of Succoth.'") — the divine oracle begins: bĕqōdshô (from his holiness/holy place — the sanctuary or the divine character of holiness from which the oracle comes). ʾĔʿlōzāh: I will exult (the victorious king's triumph shout). Shechem (central Canaan, near Nablus) and Succoth (in Transjordan) are named as the divine king's domains — territories on both sides of the Jordan representing the full extent of the promised land.
8: לִי גִלְעָד לִי מְנַשֶּׁה וְאֶפְרַיִם מָעוֹז רֹאשִׁי יְהוּדָה מְחֹקְקִי (lî gilʿād lî mĕnashsheh wĕʾeprayim māʿôz rōʾshî yĕhûdāh mĕḥōqĕqî, "Gilead belongs to me; Manasseh belongs to me; Ephraim is the helmet on my head; Judah is my royal scepter!") — the divine king's territorial sovereignty declared through possession formulae. Māʿôz rōʾshî: the strength/fortress of my head (the helmet — Ephraim as the military power protecting the royal head). Mĕḥōqĕqî: my lawgiver/scepter (Judah as the seat of royal authority — Genesis 49:10: "the scepter shall not depart from Judah... until tribute comes to him"). The tribal territories become metaphors for the divine king's own equipment.
9: מוֹאָב סִיר רַחְצִי עַל-אֱדוֹם אַשְׁלִיךְ נַעֲלִי עֲלֵי-פְלֶשֶׁת אֶתְרוֹעָע (môʾāb sîr raḥṣî ʿal-ʾĕdôm ʾashlîk naʿălî ʿălê-pĕleshet ʾetroʿāʿ, "Moab is my washbasin; over Edom I toss my sandal; over Philistia I raise a victory shout!") — the three traditional enemies of Israel subjected to symbolic gestures of contempt. Sîr raḥṣî: washbasin/pot for washing (the vessel used to wash feet — a menial, domestic image: Moab the great neighboring kingdom reduced to a servant's foot-washing bowl). ʾAshlîk naʿălî: I throw my sandal (to throw a sandal was either an act of possession-taking — Ruth 4:7-8 — or contemptuous dismissal — "this is only worth the throw of a shoe"). Philistia receives the victory-shout of the conquering warrior.
10: מִי יֹבִלֵנִי עִיר מִבְצָר מִי נָחַנִי עַד-אֱדוֹם (mî yôbilênî ʿîr mibṣār mî nāḥanî ʿad-ʾĕdôm, "Who will take me into the strong, walled city? Who will guide me all the way to Edom?") — the transition from divine oracle (vv7-9) back to human prayer: if God has declared these territories his, who will actually lead the human king into them? ʿÎr mibṣār: the fortified city (the inaccessible stronghold — Psalms 60:9: the same question). The gap between the divine promise and the human reality creates the space of petition.
11: הֲלֹא אַתָּה אֱלֹהִים זְנַחְתָּנוּ וְלֹא-תֵצֵא אֱלֹהִים בְּצִבְאוֹתֵינוּ (hălōʾ ʾattāh ʾĕlōhîm zĕnaḥtānû wĕlōʾ-têṣêʾ ʾĕlōhîm bĕṣibʾōtênû, "God, have you not turned your back on us? Why do you no longer march out with our armies?") — zānah: to reject, to spurn (the covenant-abandonment word — Psalms 44:9,23; Lamentations 3:31: "the Lord will not cast off forever"). Lōʾ-têṣêʾ bĕṣibʾōtênû: you do not go out with our armies (God as the divine warrior who marched at the head of Israel's army — Exodus 13:21; Judges 4:14; 2 Samuel 5:24). The divine oracle of vv7-9 is believed — the sovereignty of God over the nations is confessed — but the present military reality contradicts it. The psalm holds the tension without resolving it.
12: הָבָה-לָּנוּ עֶזְרָת מִצָּר וְשָׁוְא תְּשׁוּעַת אָדָם (hābāh-llānû ʿezrāt miṣṣār wĕshāwĕʾ tĕshûʿat ʾādām, "Come to our aid against the enemy — human help amounts to nothing!") — shāwĕʾ tĕshûʿat ʾādām: "vain/emptiness is the salvation of man" (shāwĕʾ: emptiness, vanity — the word for the name taken in vain, Exodus 20:7; the vanity of Ecclesiastes is hebel but shāwĕʾ denotes deliberate falseness/uselessness). Psalms 60:11: the identical phrase. Human military assistance is not merely insufficient but fundamentally unreliable — the word "vain" suggests something that promises but does not deliver. The petition for divine help is thus grounded in the acknowledged bankruptcy of human alternatives.
13: בֵּאלֹהִים נַעֲשֶׂה-חָיִל וְהוּא יָבוּס צָרֵינוּ (bêʾlōhîm naʿăśeh-ḥāyil wĕhûʾ yābûs ṣārênû, "With God we will fight with courage, for he is the one who will crush our enemies underfoot!") — the psalm's final confidence: bêʾlōhîm naʿăśeh-ḥāyil (with/through God we will do valiantly — ḥayil: valor, military strength). Not instead of human action but through human action empowered by God. Yābûs ṣārênû: "he will trample our adversaries" (bûs: to tread underfoot — Psalms 44:5; Micah 7:19; Malachi 4:3). Psalms 60:12: the identical confidence. The composite psalm ends where it must: not in human calculation but in divine victory.
